title_en: The Five Universal Maxims and Their Scope
content_en:
The five universal legal maxims (al-qawa'id al-kulliyyah al-khams) are the foundation of the qawa'id fiqhiyyah literature and the starting point for understanding Ibn Rajab's contribution. These five maxims are accepted across all four major schools of Sunni jurisprudence — Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali — and together they describe the master logic of Islamic law as a system.

The first maxim — 'Actions are judged by their intentions' (al-umur bi-maqasidiha) — is derived from the well-known hadith of Umar ibn al-Khattab: 'Actions are by intentions, and every person will have what they intended.' This maxim establishes that the moral and legal status of an act is inseparable from the intention behind it. The same outward act may be obligatory, recommended, permissible, or even prohibited depending on the intention. A person who gives money can be performing charity, paying a debt, seeking praise, or bribing an official — the external act is identical, but the legal and moral status differs entirely based on the intention. This maxim has an enormous number of applications across all domains of fiqh and is among the most frequently cited.

The second maxim — 'Certainty is not removed by doubt' (al-yaqin la yazul bil-shak) — governs the vast domain of legal presumptions. If a person is certain they have performed wudu (ritual purification) and then doubts whether they have subsequently passed wind, the default presumption of purity stands — certainty is not removed by doubt. This principle protects legal certainty and prevents the paralysis of constant doubt about one's ritual status, contractual obligations, or other legal states.

The third maxim — 'Hardship brings ease' (al-mashaqqah tajlib al-taysir) — encodes the Islamic law's systematic provision for reducing obligations under genuine hardship. The Quran states 'God intends ease for you and does not intend hardship' (2:185). This maxim explains the logic behind the entire structure of Islamic legal concessions (rukhas): the permission to combine and shorten prayers while traveling, to break fasting in illness, to eat prohibited food in life-threatening necessity, to pray sitting or lying when standing is impossible.

The fourth maxim — 'Harm must be removed' (al-darar yuzal) — is derived from the prophetic hadith 'There shall be no harm and no reciprocating harm' (la darar wa la dirar). It establishes the removal of harm (darar) as a foundational principle of Islamic law and explains the entire domain of legal remedies: why contracts can be voided when one party is deceived, why courts can dissolve marriages that harm the wife, why defective goods can be returned.

The fifth maxim — 'Custom is an authority' (al-adah muhakkamah) — establishes that well-established custom (urf) has legal weight in Islamic jurisprudence where the Quran and Sunnah have not specified otherwise. What counts as a reasonable salary, what constitutes an adequate dwelling, what qualifies as a customary gift — these are determined partly by reference to the custom of the community. Ibn Rajab's work discusses all five of these maxims and then proceeds to the additional 155 maxims that elaborate more specific principles across the different domains of fiqh.
